fragments.content: Radiation therapy uses high-energy particles or waves to damage tumor DNA, causing cell death. External beam radiation therapy (EBRT) delivers radiation from outside the body: 3D conformal radiation therapy (3D-CRT) shapes beams to tumor; intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) varies beam intensity for complex shapes; volumetric modulated arc therapy (VMAT) delivers continuous arc rotation; stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT/SRS) delivers high-dose precise radiation in 1-5 fractions. Proton therapy uses protons with Bragg peak effect, sparing distal tissues. Brachytherapy places radioactive sources inside/near tumor: low-dose rate (LDR) permanent seeds (prostate cancer); high-dose rate (HDR) temporary catheters (cervical, breast cancer). Radiobiology principles: 4 R's - repair (normal tissue repairs sublethal damage), reassortment (cells redistribute through cell cycle), repopulation (tumor cells proliferate between fractions), reoxygenation (hypoxic cells become oxygenated, more radiosensitive).

Fractionation schedules balance tumor control and normal tissue toxicity. Conventional fractionation: 1.8-2 Gy per fraction, once daily, 5 days/week; total dose 60-70 Gy over 6-7 weeks for definitive treatment. Hypofractionation: larger doses per fraction (2.5-3 Gy), shorter overall time; standard for breast cancer (40-42.5 Gy in 15-16 fractions), prostate cancer (60 Gy in 20 fractions). SBRT: very high dose per fraction (6-20 Gy), 1-5 fractions; used for early-stage lung cancer, liver metastases, brain metastases, spine metastases. Hyperfractionation: smaller doses twice daily, higher total dose; investigated in head/neck cancer. Treatment planning: CT simulation defines target volumes (GTV, CTV, PTV); organs at risk contoured; dose constraints based on QUANTEC guidelines. Concurrent chemoradiation enhances efficacy in many cancers (head/neck, lung, cervical, anal) but increases toxicity. Adjuvant radiation post-surgery reduces local recurrence (breast, rectal, endometrial cancers).

Radiation side effects classified as acute (during/within 3 months) or late (>3 months post-treatment). Acute effects: fatigue (universal), skin reactions (erythema, desquamation), mucositis (head/neck, pelvic radiation), esophagitis (thoracic radiation), diarrhea (abdominal/pelvic), cytopenias (large bone marrow volumes irradiated). Managed with supportive care: topical steroids for skin, pain control for mucositis, antidiarrheals, nutritional support. Late effects: fibrosis (tissue scarring, organ dysfunction), lymphedema, xerostomia (permanent after head/neck radiation), cognitive decline (brain radiation), cardiac toxicity (left breast/chest radiation), secondary malignancies (sarcomas, carcinomas in field, latency 5-30 years). Organ-specific constraints: lung V20 <35% (pneumonitis risk), heart mean dose <26 Gy (cardiac events), spinal cord max <50 Gy (myelopathy), bowel V45 <195cc (obstruction risk). RTOG/EORTC toxicity criteria grade adverse events 1-5.
